text.title: Cohort Analysis Rules and Failure Modes
text.summary: Mandatory rules for valid cohort analysis: minimum cohort size, diagonal effect, survivorship bias, magic-feature fallacy, and time-zone correctness.

rule.statement: Use weekly cohorts for early-stage products; switch to monthly once signup volume exceeds 10k per week. Suppress or grey-label cohorts with fewer than 100 users.
rule.rationale: Too-granular cohorts produce wild percentage swings from small sample sizes; the trend noise overwhelms the signal.

rule.statement: Never compare D90 retention across cohorts until every cohort in the comparison has at least 90 days of history.
rule.rationale: The diagonal effect: newest cohorts have incomplete observable history. A D90 comparison that includes a 30-day-old cohort is meaningless — the cell is empty, not zero.

rule.statement: Do not use retained users to define "valuable behaviors" — filter to only users who were active at signup, not those who survived to D30, before computing behavioral cohorts.
rule.rationale: The survivorship bias / magic-feature fallacy: if you define feature users as "users who used feature X and stayed", you have already filtered out churned users who also used it. Any feature correlates with retention when computed this way.

rule.statement: Use explicit timezone and a date floor in all cohort SQL; a signup at 23:55 UTC and a first event at 00:05 UTC creates a phantom D1 event — enforce UTC floor(event_date) = floor(signup_date) for day 0.
rule.rationale: Timezone bugs are the most common source of incorrect D1 retention inflating by 10–30%.

antipattern.description: Confusing "retained" (still active in window) with "returned" (came back on exact day N).
antipattern.reason: The two definitions produce very different numbers; always pin the definition in the prompt and verify the SQL.

antipattern.description: Using SUM where COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) is needed.
antipattern.reason: SUM counts events, not users; a multi-event session inflates the count. Agents default to SUM in naive SQL.

antipattern.description: Claiming a behavioral cohort finding proves causation ("feature X caused the lift").
antipattern.reason: Behavioral cohort analysis is correlational — redirect to ab-testing-basics for causal claims.
